lesson.intro: Every query starts with SELECT (which columns you want) and FROM (which table they live in). SELECT * grabs every column, but naming columns explicitly — SELECT name, attack — is almost always better: you get exactly what you asked for, in the order you asked for it.
lesson.exampleSql: SELECT name, type1, attack FROM pokemon
lesson.wrapUp: You now own the two clauses every query starts with. SELECT chooses columns, FROM chooses the table — and explicit column lists beat SELECT * because they make intent obvious and results stable. Everything else in SQL decorates this skeleton.

lesson.intro: WHERE keeps only the rows that pass a test. Combine tests with AND / OR, compare with = <> > < >= <=, match text with LIKE. One trap: NULL never equals anything — use IS NULL / IS NOT NULL for missing values (many Pokémon have no second type).
lesson.exampleSql: SELECT name, attack FROM pokemon WHERE type1 = 'fire' AND attack >= 100
lesson.wrapUp: Filtering is half of analysis: WHERE keeps rows that pass your test, AND/OR combine tests, and NULL passes no test at all — remember IS NULL when data is missing. From here on, almost every query you write will carry a WHERE.

lesson.intro: ORDER BY sorts the output (ASC by default, DESC for highest-first) and LIMIT keeps the first N rows — together they answer every 'top 10' question. Sort by multiple columns to break ties deterministically: ORDER BY total DESC, name means 'by total, then alphabetically among equals'.
lesson.exampleSql: SELECT name, speed FROM pokemon ORDER BY speed DESC, name LIMIT 5
lesson.wrapUp: ORDER BY makes results deterministic; LIMIT keeps them digestible; together they answer every 'top N' question. The tiebreaker habit — ORDER BY total DESC, name — separates queries that are reproducible from ones that shuffle under your feet.

lesson.intro: DISTINCT collapses duplicate rows in the result. SELECT DISTINCT type1 lists each primary type once; COUNT(DISTINCT type1) counts how many different values exist. DISTINCT applies to the whole selected row, not just the column it sits next to.
lesson.exampleSql: SELECT DISTINCT type1 FROM pokemon ORDER BY type1
lesson.wrapUp: DISTINCT collapses duplicate result rows, and COUNT(DISTINCT col) counts unique values — your go-to for 'how many different…' questions. Remember it applies to the whole selected row: DISTINCT a, b means unique combinations.

lesson.intro: Aggregate functions squash many rows into one value: COUNT(*) counts rows, AVG/SUM/MIN/MAX do what they say. With no GROUP BY, the whole table becomes a single output row. COUNT(col) skips NULLs while COUNT(*) counts everything — that difference answers 'how many Pokémon have a second type?'
lesson.exampleSql: SELECT COUNT(*) AS pokemon_count, AVG(attack) AS avg_attack FROM pokemon
lesson.wrapUp: Aggregates fold many rows into one: COUNT, AVG, SUM, MIN, MAX. Without GROUP BY the whole table becomes a single row, and COUNT(col) skips NULLs while COUNT(*) doesn't — the foundation for the grouping you'll meet in Shaping.

lesson.intro: GROUP BY collapses rows that share a value into one row per group, so aggregates like COUNT and AVG report per group instead of once for the whole table. SELECT generation, COUNT(*) FROM pokemon GROUP BY generation returns one count for each generation. The iron rule: every column in your SELECT must either sit inside an aggregate or appear in the GROUP BY — the grouping columns are what identify each output row. You can group by more than one column, like generation and type1 together, to get finer buckets.
lesson.exampleSql: SELECT type1, COUNT(*) AS pokemon_count, ROUND(AVG(attack), 1) AS avg_attack FROM pokemon GROUP BY type1 ORDER BY pokemon_count DESC
lesson.wrapUp: GROUP BY turns table-wide aggregates into per-group answers — one row per generation, per type, or per combination of both. Keep the iron rule in mind: every selected column is either aggregated or in the GROUP BY. Next you'll filter these groups themselves with HAVING.

lesson.intro: WHERE filters individual rows before grouping; HAVING filters whole groups after aggregating. To keep only the primary types with at least 50 Pokémon you can't use WHERE — that count doesn't exist until the group is formed — so you write GROUP BY type1 HAVING COUNT(*) >= 50. HAVING can test any aggregate: HAVING AVG(total) > 430, HAVING MAX(attack) >= 150. WHERE and HAVING often work together — WHERE trims the rows first, then HAVING trims the resulting groups.
lesson.exampleSql: SELECT type1, COUNT(*) AS pokemon_count FROM pokemon GROUP BY type1 HAVING COUNT(*) >= 50 ORDER BY pokemon_count DESC
lesson.wrapUp: HAVING is WHERE for groups — it tests aggregates that don't exist until grouping has happened. Remember the pipeline order: WHERE filters rows, GROUP BY forms groups, HAVING filters groups. With grouping mastered, you're ready to reshape values row by row with CASE.

lesson.intro: CASE is SQL's if/else: it walks its WHEN tests in order and returns the first THEN whose condition passes, or the ELSE value if none do. On the Yu-Gi-Oh cards table you can turn a raw level into a tier — CASE WHEN level <= 4 THEN 'Low' WHEN level <= 8 THEN 'Mid' ELSE 'High' END — then GROUP BY that label to count each tier. A CASE expression fits almost anywhere a column can: in SELECT, in GROUP BY, even in ORDER BY to build a custom sort. Wrapped in SUM it becomes a conditional counter — SUM(CASE WHEN atk >= 3000 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) tallies only the heavy hitters.
lesson.exampleSql: SELECT CASE WHEN level <= 4 THEN 'Low' WHEN level <= 8 THEN 'Mid' ELSE 'High' END AS level_tier, COUNT(*) AS card_count FROM cards WHERE level IS NOT NULL GROUP BY level_tier ORDER BY card_count DESC
lesson.wrapUp: CASE turns raw values into labels, buckets, and conditional counts, and it slots into SELECT, GROUP BY, or ORDER BY. This is your first tool on the Yu-Gi-Oh cards table — next you'll reshape the text of the card names themselves with string functions.

lesson.intro: String functions reshape text. LENGTH(name) gives a card name's character count; UPPER(name) and LOWER(name) change its case; SUBSTR(name, 1, 1) pulls out a slice — here the first character; REPLACE(name, '-', ' ') swaps every hyphen for a space. You stitch strings together with the || operator: name || ' (' || type || ')' builds a label like "Dark Magician (Normal Monster)". Because these all return ordinary values, you can sort by them, group by them, or feed them into COUNT.
lesson.exampleSql: SELECT name, LENGTH(name) AS name_length FROM cards ORDER BY LENGTH(name) DESC, name LIMIT 5
lesson.wrapUp: String functions let you measure, slice, recase, and stitch text — and their outputs behave like any other column inside ORDER BY, GROUP BY, or an aggregate. You've now shaped both numbers (CASE) and text; the final Shaping skill confronts the values that aren't there at all — NULL.

lesson.intro: NULL means "no value," and it runs all through Yu-Gi-Oh's data: Spell and Trap cards have NULL atk, def, and level; Link Monsters have NULL def and level; and some legendary cards — like Slifer the Sky Dragon and The Winged Dragon of Ra — have variable "?" ATK, stored as NULL. NULL is not zero and not empty — any comparison with it (atk = NULL) returns unknown, so you must test with IS NULL / IS NOT NULL. Aggregates quietly skip NULLs: COUNT(atk) counts only the cards that have one, so COUNT(*) - COUNT(atk) reveals how many are missing it, and AVG(atk) averages only the real values. COALESCE(atk, 0) substitutes a fallback, letting you fold the missing values back in when you want them to count.
lesson.exampleSql: SELECT COUNT(*) AS total_cards, COUNT(atk) AS with_atk, COUNT(*) - COUNT(atk) AS missing_atk FROM cards
lesson.wrapUp: NULL is absence, not zero: test it with IS NULL, and remember that COUNT and AVG skip it while COALESCE supplies a stand-in. Handling missing data deliberately is what separates queries that quietly mislead from ones you can trust. That completes Shaping — you can now group, filter groups, branch with CASE, reshape text, and tame NULLs.

lesson.intro: INNER JOIN stitches two tables together on a matching key, keeping only the rows that pair up on both sides. In the Yu-Gi-Oh data, cards.id matches card_sets.card_id, so joining them pairs each card with every set it was printed in — one result row per printing. You give each table an alias (cards c, card_sets cs) and state the match in ON: FROM cards c JOIN card_sets cs ON c.id = cs.card_id, after which columns from either side are yours to SELECT, filter, group, or count. The same key joins cards to banlist (c.id = b.card_id) to pair a card with its restriction history. Cards with no match simply vanish from an INNER JOIN — that dropping-out is the whole reason the LEFT JOIN you meet next exists.
lesson.exampleSql: SELECT c.name, cs.set_name, cs.rarity FROM cards c JOIN card_sets cs ON c.id = cs.card_id WHERE c.name = 'Dark Magician' ORDER BY cs.set_code
lesson.wrapUp: INNER JOIN pairs rows across tables on a key and keeps only the matches, letting one query span cards, their printings, and their banlist entries at once. Because unmatched rows drop out, an inner join quietly filters too. Next you'll use LEFT JOIN to keep those unmatched rows and see exactly what's missing.

lesson.intro: A LEFT JOIN keeps every row from the left table even when the right table has no match — the right-side columns just come back NULL. That makes it the tool for 'what's missing' questions: LEFT JOIN card_sets, and cards that were never printed appear with a NULL card_sets side, which you isolate with WHERE cs.card_id IS NULL. Watch where conditions go: a filter in the ON clause (ON b.card_id = c.id AND b.format = 'tcg') is applied as part of the match and preserves unmatched rows, while that same test in WHERE would silently collapse the LEFT JOIN back into an inner one. COUNT is careful here too — COUNT(cs.card_id) tallies only real matches, reporting 0 for a card with no printing, whereas COUNT(*) would wrongly report 1. COALESCE(status, 'Unrestricted') swaps a friendly label in for the NULLs.
lesson.exampleSql: SELECT c.name, cs.set_name FROM cards c LEFT JOIN card_sets cs ON c.id = cs.card_id WHERE c.archetype = 'Blue-Eyes' ORDER BY c.name, cs.set_name
lesson.wrapUp: LEFT JOIN keeps unmatched left rows with NULLs on the right, turning 'which cards have no printing or no banlist entry' into a WHERE ... IS NULL filter and COUNT(column) into an honest zero. Keep the match conditions in ON and post-filters in WHERE and you control exactly which rows survive. Next you'll stack whole result sets with UNION, INTERSECT, and EXCEPT.

lesson.intro: A self-join joins a table to itself by listing it twice under different aliases — the same JOIN mechanic, both sides drawn from one table. In the Pokémon data, evolves_from names each species' pre-evolution, so pokemon p JOIN pokemon pre ON p.evolves_from = pre.name pairs each evolved Pokémon with the row of its pre-evolution — when that pre-evolution exists as a row under the same name — letting you compare their stats or types side by side. Chain a third copy to reach a grandparent (pre.evolves_from = gp.name), or line up any two rows of the table you like. The identical idea reaches into a second table: pokemon p JOIN type_matchups tm ON tm.defender_type = p.type1 attaches each Pokémon to the rows describing what damages its type. Aliases are mandatory in a self-join — without them SQL cannot tell which copy of the table you mean.
lesson.exampleSql: SELECT p.name, pre.name AS evolves_from, p.attack, pre.attack AS pre_attack FROM pokemon p JOIN pokemon pre ON p.evolves_from = pre.name ORDER BY p.name
lesson.wrapUp: A self-join is just a table joined to aliased copies of itself — the trick is naming each copy so you can compare one row against another, whether that's an evolution and its pre-evolution or two links of a longer chain. The same JOIN also reaches sideways into the type-matchup chart. Next, set operations stack whole result sets instead of widening rows.

lesson.intro: Set operations stack the results of two queries that share the same columns. UNION returns rows appearing in either result with duplicates removed (UNION ALL keeps them); INTERSECT returns only rows present in both; EXCEPT returns rows in the first result that are absent from the second. They shine on the banlist, where a card may sit on the TCG list, the OCG list, or both: a Forbidden-in-TCG query INTERSECT a Forbidden-in-OCG query yields the cards banned in both worlds, and EXCEPT yields the TCG-only bans. Each side is an independent SELECT whose columns must line up in count and order, and a single trailing ORDER BY sorts the combined result.
lesson.exampleSql: SELECT c.name FROM cards c JOIN banlist b ON c.id = b.card_id WHERE b.format = 'tcg' AND b.status = 'Forbidden' INTERSECT SELECT c.name FROM cards c JOIN banlist b ON c.id = b.card_id WHERE b.format = 'ocg' AND b.status = 'Forbidden' ORDER BY name
lesson.wrapUp: UNION, INTERSECT, and EXCEPT combine two same-shaped result sets into their union, overlap, or difference — ideal for comparing the TCG and OCG banlists. The columns must align and only a trailing ORDER BY sorts the whole. Next you'll nest one query inside another with subqueries.

lesson.intro: A subquery is a query nested inside another. A scalar subquery returns a single value to compare against — WHERE atk > (SELECT AVG(atk) FROM cards WHERE atk IS NOT NULL) keeps only above-average attackers, computing that average once. An IN subquery returns a column of values to test membership: WHERE id IN (SELECT card_id FROM banlist WHERE format = 'tcg') keeps cards on the TCG banlist, and NOT IN keeps those absent from it. A subquery in HAVING lets a grouped query compare each group's aggregate against a table-wide figure. These inner queries run against the same tables and stand entirely on their own — you can always run one by itself to check it first.
lesson.exampleSql: SELECT name, atk FROM cards WHERE atk > (SELECT AVG(atk) FROM cards WHERE atk IS NOT NULL) AND archetype = 'Blue-Eyes' ORDER BY atk DESC, name
lesson.wrapUp: Subqueries let one query feed another: a scalar subquery supplies a single comparison value, an IN or NOT IN subquery supplies a membership list, and a HAVING subquery compares each group to a global figure. Each inner query is self-contained. When the inner query needs to reference the current outer row, you reach for the correlated subqueries you'll meet next.

lesson.intro: A correlated subquery references the outer row, so it is re-evaluated for each row instead of once. WHERE atk > (SELECT AVG(atk) FROM cards c2 WHERE c2.race = c.race) compares every card to the average ATK of its own race — the inner query depends on c.race from the outer card. EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM card_sets cs WHERE cs.card_id = c.id) is true when at least one matching printing exists, and NOT EXISTS is the anti-join in subquery form: cards with no printing at all. Correlated subqueries express the per-row, per-group comparisons a flat query can't — 'above its race's average,' 'the top ATK in its race,' 'Forbidden in the OCG but nowhere on the TCG list.' They are powerful but re-run for every outer row, which is exactly the repeated work the next region streamlines.
lesson.exampleSql: SELECT name, race, atk FROM cards c WHERE atk IS NOT NULL AND atk > (SELECT AVG(atk) FROM cards c2 WHERE c2.race = c.race AND c2.atk IS NOT NULL) AND race = 'Dragon' ORDER BY atk DESC, name
lesson.wrapUp: A correlated subquery re-runs for every outer row, wiring the inner query to that row's values — the engine behind 'above its own group's average' and EXISTS / NOT EXISTS membership tests. That per-row power costs repeated work. In the Analyst region, CTEs name a result once and window functions compute across groups in a single pass, replacing many of these patterns.

lesson.intro: A CTE (Common Table Expression) is a named query you define with WITH before your main query, letting you build a result in readable, reusable steps instead of one dense nested statement. In the Digimon world you might name a step vaccine_mon that selects every Digimon whose attribute is 'Vaccine', then SELECT from it as if it were a table — even joining it to the evolutions table on id = from_id to trace what those Digimon evolve into. You can chain several CTEs separated by commas, and each may reference the ones before it, so a three-part question becomes three plainly named steps. Because a CTE can hold a grouped, aggregated result, you can count Digimon per attribute in one step and then keep only the crowded attributes in the next — filtering on a count that didn't exist until the group was formed.
lesson.exampleSql: WITH by_attribute AS (SELECT attribute, COUNT(*) AS digimon_count FROM digimon WHERE attribute IS NOT NULL GROUP BY attribute) SELECT attribute, digimon_count FROM by_attribute WHERE digimon_count >= 100 ORDER BY digimon_count DESC, attribute
lesson.wrapUp: A CTE names an intermediate result so a complex question reads as a sequence of clear steps — and unlike a subquery buried in a WHERE, it can be joined, reused, and stacked. This is the readable backbone of every analytical query to come. Next you'll add window functions, which compute across rows without collapsing them — starting by ranking Digimon within their own attribute or level.

lesson.intro: A window function computes across a set of related rows while keeping every row — no collapsing the way GROUP BY does. RANK(), DENSE_RANK(), and ROW_NUMBER() each number rows within a partition defined by OVER (PARTITION BY ... ORDER BY ...): here, PARTITION BY attribute ORDER BY release_year DESC ranks each Digimon against others sharing its attribute, newest first. The three differ only in how they treat ties: ROW_NUMBER always gives distinct numbers (1, 2, 3, 4), RANK lets ties share a number then skips the next (1, 1, 3), and DENSE_RANK shares without skipping (1, 1, 2); NTILE(4) instead slices the ordered rows into four roughly equal buckets, the tool for quartiles. Ordinary aggregates ride the same machinery too: COUNT(*) OVER (PARTITION BY attribute) stamps every row with its group's size without collapsing anything. Because a window function can't sit in a WHERE clause, you compute it in a CTE and filter on the result — that's how 'the newest Digimon per attribute' or 'top 3 per level' is done. And since many Digimon share a release_year, add a tiebreaker like name inside the OVER clause when you need one definite ordering.
lesson.exampleSql: WITH ranked AS (SELECT attribute, name, release_year, ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY attribute ORDER BY release_year DESC, name) AS recency_rank FROM digimon WHERE attribute IS NOT NULL) SELECT attribute, name, release_year FROM ranked WHERE recency_rank = 1 ORDER BY attribute
lesson.wrapUp: Ranking windows number rows within a partition, and the choice between ROW_NUMBER, RANK, and DENSE_RANK is entirely about how ties are handled, while NTILE splits an ordering into buckets. Filter the ranked result in an outer query to get 'top N per group', and pin tiebreakers inside OVER so the ordering is never left to chance. Next you'll shift from ranking to reaching across rows — comparing each period to the one before it with LAG and LEAD over Seattle's 311 service requests.

lesson.intro: LAG and LEAD reach across rows to pull a neighbor's value onto the current row — exactly what period-over-period comparisons need. This is your first query against Seattle's 311 data: the requests table, one row per service request with a created_date. There is no closed-date column here, so the analysis is a time series — bucket requests into days, weeks, or months with DATE_TRUNC, COUNT them, and then LAG(reqs) OVER (ORDER BY mon) hands you the previous period's count so you can compute a delta. LAG(reqs, 7) looks seven rows back, LEAD(reqs) looks one ahead, and PARTITION BY department keeps each department's series separate so one department's first month doesn't borrow another's last. One trap: WHERE runs before window functions, so when you need to compare against a period outside your display range, compute the offset in a CTE over the full series and filter afterward.
lesson.exampleSql: WITH monthly AS (SELECT CAST(DATE_TRUNC('month', created_date) AS DATE) AS mon, COUNT(*) AS reqs FROM requests GROUP BY 1) SELECT mon, reqs, reqs - LAG(reqs) OVER (ORDER BY mon) AS delta FROM monthly ORDER BY mon
lesson.wrapUp: LAG and LEAD turn a column of period counts into deltas, growth rates, and same-day-last-week comparisons by borrowing a neighboring row's value. Bucket with DATE_TRUNC, partition to keep series apart, and remember that filtering before a window can starve it of the rows it needs to look back on. Next, window frames aggregate a sliding band of neighbors instead of reaching for a single one.

lesson.intro: A window frame aggregates a moving band of rows around the current one, defined by a ROWS BETWEEN clause inside OVER. On the 311 daily counts, AVG(reqs) OVER (ORDER BY dt ROWS BETWEEN 6 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW) is a rolling 7-day average — the current day plus the six before it — which smooths out the weekday-versus-weekend swing a single LAG can't. Change the frame to change the question: ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW is a running total, ROWS BETWEEN 2 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW is a 3-period moving average, and an empty OVER () spans every row — handy as the denominator for a cumulative percentage. AVG over a window returns a DOUBLE, so wrap rolling averages in ROUND(..., 2) rather than casting to DECIMAL. And as with offsets, WHERE filters before the window — compute the frame over the full series in a CTE, then filter to your display range so even the earliest shown rows have a complete window behind them.
lesson.exampleSql: WITH daily AS (SELECT created_date AS dt, COUNT(*) AS reqs FROM requests GROUP BY 1) SELECT dt, reqs, ROUND(AVG(reqs) OVER (ORDER BY dt ROWS BETWEEN 6 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW), 2) AS rolling_avg_7d FROM daily ORDER BY dt LIMIT 10
lesson.wrapUp: A frame clause turns a window into a sliding aggregate — rolling averages, running totals, and cumulative shares all come from choosing which neighboring rows ROWS BETWEEN includes. Pair frames with PARTITION BY to keep separate series apart, and ROUND fractional averages instead of casting them. One region skill remains: recursive CTEs, which let a query walk a chain of rows step by step.

lesson.intro: A recursive CTE queries a hierarchy by referencing itself, walking a chain one link at a time. It has two parts joined by UNION ALL: an anchor that picks the starting rows, and a recursive step that joins the CTE back to the base table to reach the next level, repeating until nothing new is found. In the Pokémon data each row's evolves_from names its pre-evolution, so anchoring on a base (evolves_from IS NULL) and stepping down to children (p.evolves_from = cte.name) walks an entire evolution family; carry a depth counter to number the stages and a base column to tag which family each member belongs to. Reverse the join (p.name = cte.evolves_from) to climb upward from a final form back to its base instead. One data quirk to respect: three form-suffixed rows name a pre-evolution that isn't itself in the table, so they never appear in any base-rooted walk — a reminder that recursion only ever reaches links that actually resolve.
lesson.exampleSql: WITH RECURSIVE family AS (SELECT name, 1 AS depth FROM pokemon WHERE name = 'charmander' UNION ALL SELECT p.name, f.depth + 1 FROM pokemon p JOIN family f ON p.evolves_from = f.name) SELECT name, depth FROM family ORDER BY depth, name
lesson.wrapUp: A recursive CTE walks a self-referencing hierarchy by repeatedly joining its own output back to the table — anchor to start, recursive step to advance, a depth counter to number the hops. Evolution chains, org charts, and threaded comments all yield to the same pattern. That completes the Analyst Power region: you can now name intermediate results, rank and offset and frame across rows, and traverse hierarchies — the full analytical toolkit.

lesson.intro: This is an arena: no new syntax, just the full case. You are the analyst for a Yu-Gi-Oh! tournament committee weighing banlist changes, and every question here is a real report spanning the three tables you already know — cards, card_sets, and banlist. Expect to decompose each one: a CTE or two to build the clean working set (say, TCG banlist entries joined to their archetypes), conditional tallies with SUM(CASE WHEN ...), and a window like RANK() OVER (ORDER BY ...) to place each row against its peers. Nothing below needs a tool you haven't already mastered across Shaping, Combining, and Analyst Power — the whole challenge is composing them. Read the goal, name your steps, then build.
lesson.exampleSql: WITH tcg_bans AS (SELECT c.archetype, b.status FROM cards c JOIN banlist b ON c.id = b.card_id WHERE b.format = 'tcg' AND c.archetype IS NOT NULL) SELECT archetype, COUNT(*) AS entries, RANK() OVER (ORDER BY COUNT(*) DESC) AS pressure_rank FROM tcg_bans GROUP BY archetype HAVING COUNT(*) >= 3 ORDER BY entries DESC, archetype
lesson.wrapUp: Case closed. You turned three flat tables into a banlist-pressure briefing by composing joins, conditional aggregation, and windows — the exact workflow a working analyst runs. The syntax was never the hard part; choosing which intermediate results to build was. Carry that decomposition instinct into the next arena.

lesson.intro: Another arena, another real brief. You are the curator of the Digimon Analyzer, the field-guide database behind every Digivice, and each question here is a completion report spanning the two tables you know — digimon (one row per Digimon, with its level, type, attribute, x_antibody flag, and release_year) and evolutions (directed edges from one form to its possible next forms). Every question is a small case study: a stated goal, hard constraints, and an exact output shape. You'll lean on CTEs to stage the working set, conditional tallies with SUM(CASE WHEN ...), joins across the evolution graph, and window functions to rank Digimon within their attribute or to reach from one debut year to the next. One caution: the evolution graph contains cycles, so there is no recursive walk to attempt — the whole challenge is composing joins, aggregation, and windows. Decompose the goal into named steps and go.
lesson.exampleSql: WITH stats AS (SELECT attribute, COUNT(*) AS total, SUM(CASE WHEN x_antibody THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS x_variants FROM digimon WHERE attribute IS NOT NULL GROUP BY attribute HAVING COUNT(*) >= 20) SELECT attribute, total, x_variants, RANK() OVER (ORDER BY total DESC) AS roster_rank FROM stats ORDER BY total DESC, attribute
lesson.wrapUp: Case closed. You turned two flat tables into a field-guide completion briefing by composing joins across the evolution graph, conditional aggregation, and windows — the exact workflow a working analyst runs. The syntax was never the hard part; choosing which intermediate results to build was. One arena remains.

lesson.intro: The final arena, and a purely operational one. You are the analyst in Seattle's 311 dispatch center, and the requests table is your entire world — one row per service request, with a created_date but no closure date, so every question here is a time series. Bucket requests into days, weeks, or months with DATE_TRUNC; then reach across rows with LAG for period-over-period change, aggregate a sliding band with a ROWS BETWEEN frame for rolling averages, and rank or bucket departments and neighborhoods with windows and CASE. Remember the frame trap you learned: WHERE runs before a window, so compute rolling metrics over the full series in a CTE and filter to your display range afterward. No entities to collect here — the badge is the prize. Read the brief and build.
lesson.exampleSql: WITH daily AS (SELECT created_date AS dt, COUNT(*) AS reqs FROM requests GROUP BY created_date) SELECT dt, reqs, ROUND(AVG(reqs) OVER (ORDER BY dt ROWS BETWEEN 6 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW), 2) AS rolling_7d FROM daily ORDER BY dt LIMIT 10
lesson.wrapUp: Case closed, and with it the whole campaign. You smoothed noisy daily counts into rolling averages, measured momentum with LAG, and ranked departments and neighborhoods into severity tiers — the standard toolkit of an operations analyst, composed under real constraints. You began with SELECT and FROM; you now decompose open-ended business questions into staged, windowed, deterministic queries. That is the difference between knowing SQL and analyzing with it. Well dueled.

lesson.intro: You've spent every region writing queries against data that was already sitting in a table. This track flips the question: how does the data get there, and who decides the shape of the pipe? Public-safety systems make that vivid. A Computer-Aided Dispatch (CAD) platform emits an unbounded stream of 911 calls and unit-status changes — faster than any warehouse wants to be written to, sometimes out of order, sometimes the very same event twice after a restart. These drills are decision drills: no SQL to run, just the judgment calls an ingestion engineer makes at the front door. How do you absorb a 10x storm surge without dropping a single record? How do you keep a live dispatch map fresh while still landing everything for next quarter's analysis? How do you stay correct when a message is redelivered or a field appears that nobody warned you about? Pick the option a working data engineer would defend in review.
lesson.wrapUp: The front-door playbook, in five moves: buffer bursts behind a durable, replayable log instead of coupling producers to consumers; split the low-latency operational path from the heavy analytical one so reporting never threatens the live system; make ingestion idempotent with a stable key so redeliveries can't inflate your counts; land the raw payload first so a new vendor field is captured, never a cause of data loss; and window on event time with a watermark so late arrivals land in the moment they actually happened. Next you'll decide where all this data lives and for how long.

lesson.intro: Once the stream is safely landed, two questions decide your architecture: where does the data physically live, and for how long is it allowed to? In the public sector the second question has teeth that commercial data rarely does. Automated License Plate Reader (ALPR) reads must be purged on a legal clock. Body-worn-camera footage runs to petabytes with tiered retention rules. Criminal Justice Information (CJI) is governed by the FBI's CJIS Security Policy. Crime statistics have to roll up into the national NIBRS standard. Each of these is really a physical-design decision — partitioning, indexing, storage tiers, encryption, dimensional modeling — chosen to fit a legal or access reality rather than raw performance alone. These drills ask you to match the design to the constraint: the layout that makes a 90-day purge a one-line operation, the index that serves the way investigators actually search, the storage class that survives an audit. Choose the answer that a data engineer would still defend when the lawyer is in the room.
lesson.wrapUp: Match physical design to the rule, not just the benchmark: partition by time so a retention cap becomes a partition drop instead of a billion-row delete; index and cluster for the geo-plus-time way records are actually queried; tier cold video to cheap archive while a legal-hold class protects evidence from early expiry; encrypt CJI at rest behind least-privilege access and immutable audit trails; and model crime statistics as a star schema at the reportable grain so incident-based data aggregates cleanly. You've now reasoned about the whole path — how data arrives and where it rests — the way a systems designer does.
